Lead.description:
Set Details

  • Infernape is a common lead for offensive teams that need disruption and offensive pressure against Steel-types. Infernape matches up well against lead Metagross and Heatran. It can also disrupt the opponent's strategy with Fake Out flinching and Encore locking to enable teammates to safely make progress. However, Infernape is very frail and struggles against other common leads, including Cresselia and Gengar.

  • The choice of moveset depends on team structure. Explosion spam teams prefer powerful Blaze-boosted Heat Waves after Focus Sash gets activated, along with 1 HP Endeavors and Feint to prevent an ally's Explosion being wasted on a foe using Protect. Otherwise, Overheat is a stronger single-target attack guaranteed to OHKO Metagross without Occa Berry, and Close Combat provides better coverage for foes like Heatran and Snorlax. Encore can seal endgames by locking foes into passive moves like Protect. Encore also combines well with Fake Out, essentially forcing the opponent to not use Protect for fear of getting Encore locked. Alternative final moves include Taunt to prevent Trick Room and U-turn to maintain momentum.

  • Focus Sash is Infernape's only viable item and is required to patch up its frailty. As previously explained, it pairs nicely with Explosion spam strategies to activate Infernape's Blaze and 1 HP Endeavor.

  • The Attack investment is enough to reliably 2HKO bulky Snorlax after Sitrus Berry recovery with Close Combat and OHKO offensive Bronzong with Overheat. If Infernape doesn't run Close Combat, then a simple spread of 252 SpA / 4 SpD / 252 Spe with a Timid nature is preferred. A Speed-boosting nature is necessary to win Speed ties against opposing Infernape and beat maximum invested base 100s like Zapdos.

Teambuilding

  • Infernape is a great lead for offensive teams. It pairs well with Azelf and Choice Scarf Metagross, both of which can use Explosion reliably with Infernape's Feint support and ability to check Steel-types. As mentioned above, once Focus Sash is activated, Infernape becomes an offensive menace with Blaze-boosted Fire-type attacks and 1 HP Endeavors.

  • Infernape can also work as a lead alongside Dragon-types like Latios or Salamence. Between Dragon-, Fire-, and Fighting-type coverage, nothing in the metagame resists all 3 and the offensive output can be oppressive.

  • Infernape struggles against priority users like Bullet Punch Metagross and Extreme Speed Togekiss, which can pick it off easily once Focus Sash activates. Dragon-types like Latios and Salamence resist both its STAB types and simply 2HKO it. Finally, Cresselia and Gengar defeat it on lead, especially if they're running Icy Wind.

Offensive Support.description:
Set Details

  • Gengar is a fast and frail attacker with good supporting qualities, using Shadow Ball to hit two of the most popular Pokemon in the format, Latios and Cresselia. On top of this, it can use its deep supporting movepool to disrupt the opposing team, though it is easily beat by Trick Room and is very frail.

  • Icy Wind is able to slow down foes, allowing allies such as Latios to avoid Speed ties with opposing Latios and outrun foes such as Infernape that would otherwise outspeed it. Icy Wind lets Gengar outspeed Latios and KO it the following turn with Shadow Ball. Taunt is useful to prevent Trick Room from opposing Cresselia and Bronzong and Thunder Wave from Zapdos while also shutting down less common sets such as Calm Mind Latios.

  • Other moves that are not listed like Explosion can be used for solid damage and getting in an ally safely. Rain Dance is another option to support Ludicolo, meanwhile Perish Song can be used to remove annoying foes such as Snorlax. Will-O-Wisp can be used to weaken foes such as Rhyperior and Metagross. These moves tend to be inconsistent, however.

  • Focus Sash helps with Gengar's frailty and allows it to pretty much guarantee getting at least one attack off. Swapping it with Life Orb gives Gengar extra power, allowing it to OHKO Latios. Lum Berry can also be used as an extra precaution against Thunder Wave Pokemon like Zapdos and Cresselia, though it is generally better preserved for a different Pokemon like Metagross.

  • The given EV spread maximizes Gengar's Speed and Special Attack, making sure it is as fast and strong as possible. Maximum Speed is necessary to Speed tie opposing Gengar and Latios.

Teambuilding

  • Gengar, having a solid matchup against Latios and providing solid speed control, works best on balance teams that appreciate said speed control. Pokemon with middling Speed like Heatran can then outspeed Pokemon like Zapdos. Gengar also works well on setup spam teams, using Taunt to prevent foes like Infernape from using Encore and slowing down foes so the setup Pokemon like Suicune can sweep more easily.

  • Gengar struggles to deal meaningful damage to much of the tier, including Bronzong, Metagross, Zapdos, and Heatran. All of these Pokemon can easily take Gengar's Shadow Ball thanks to their superb bulk and retaliate for big damage or, in Bronzong's case, set Trick Room, which is something that Gengar can struggle with due to it being naturally fast. Additionally, when Trick Room is set up, sweepers like Rhyperior and Snorlax can easily KO Gengar, the latter of which is immune to Shadow Ball.

  • Allies such as Heatran can easily take down the Steel-type Pokemon that haunt Gengar. In return, Gengar hits Latios, which Heatran can sometimes struggle against, hard. Latios helps against Zapdos, eating all its attacks and hitting it hard with Psychic or Draco Meteor, while allies like Swampert are able to quash common Trick Room plans, notably threatening Rhyperior with Water-type attacks.

Bulky Attacker.description:
Set Details

  • Heatran is a premier Fire-type attacker in the VGC 2009 metagame. With a great Special Attack stat, Heatran offensively checks common threats such as Metagross, Abomasnow, and Bronzong. Heatran also has excellent natural bulk and a great defensive typing, providing a defensive answer to Latios, Cresselia, and Gengar.

  • Heatran's high natural bulk allows it to stay on the field and progressively wear down foes with Heat Wave.

  • Heatran also has a quite respectable Speed stat of 77, being slightly faster than Metagross and Smeargle. The second EV spread leverages Heatran's Speed to set up Substitute against slower Pokemon such as the aforementioned Metagross and Smeargle, in addition to Pokemon who commonly lack threatening offensive tools against it such as Cresselia and Gengar.

  • Earth Power is almost mandatory for hitting Pokemon resistant or immune to Fire-type attacks such as Infernape, Rhyperior, and especially opposing Heatran. Significant Speed investment and Earth Power are extremely important in the mirror matchup.

  • Pokemon that cannot hit Heatran for good damage such as Cresselia, Bronzong, Gengar, and Abomasnow, provide a perfect opportunity for Heatran to set up a Substitute. Substitute can block status moves such as Cresselia's Thunder Wave or Smeargle's Dark Void in addition to stalling Trick Room turns. Heatran can also shut down status moves from supportive Pokemon for its teammates with Taunt. Trick Room-centered teams are especially vulnerable to Taunt Heatran.

  • Hidden Power Ice can also be quite effective on Shuca Berry variants, enabling Heatran to hit the likes of Latios, Garchomp, and Zapdos for super effective damage and survive any Ground-type moves they may have.

  • Overheat can OHKO most Metagross and Bronzong without Occa Berry. Overheat is also very good at punching holes through the opponent's team, breaking through Pokemon such as Cresselia and bulky Zapdos.

  • Shuca Berry allows Heatran to survive essentially every important Ground-type attack, such as Metagross's and Focus Sash Garchomp's Earthquake, and even set up a Substitute after. Leftovers is great for extending Heatran's longevity on Substitute sets. When behind a Substitute and using Leftovers, Heatran becomes very hard to remove for teams that rely on Psychic-, Steel-, or Dragon-type attacks.

  • 68 Speed EVs are needed for Heatran to outspeed uninvested base 85 Speed Pokemon such as Cresselia and Suicune.

  • 252 HP and 20 Defense EVs allow Heatran to survive Close Combat from Jolly Infernape.

  • 164 Special Attack EVs with a Modest nature enable Heatran to 2HKO most Metagross with Heat Wave, even if they are holding Occa Berry.

  • 212 HP EVs maximizes healing from Leftovers, which helps Heatran set up more Substitutes.

Teambuilding

  • Because of Heatran's flexible Speed stat, common speed control tools such as Icy Wind, Thunder Wave, and Trick Room can all work in Heatran's favor depending on the team.

  • Skill Swap in combination with Levitate is also an extremely powerful strategy. Granting Levitate to Heatran can essentially make it unkillable in combination with Substitute + Leftovers for certain teams that rely on Ground-type attacks to KO Heatran consistently.

  • Teams that struggle with dealing consistent damage to Metagross and Cresselia really appreciate Heatran's ability to set up Substitute in front of them and attack with Heat Wave.

  • Even with Shuca Berry, most Ground-type attacks will 2HKO Heatran. Fast Ground-types such as Garchomp are extremely tough to handle when leading Heatran.

  • Snorlax is one of Heatran's primary defensive checks, as it has great HP and Special Defense in combination with Thick Fat. While some Snorlax that carry Crunch and Fire Punch may not be able to hit Heatran back for good damage, most carry Earthquake.

  • Bulky Waters such as Gyarados and Suicune are similarly not afraid of Heat Wave or Earth Power and can deal super effective damage back.

  • Out of the Water-types, Swampert is the most fearsome, as few Pokemon carry Grass-type moves to hit Swampert for super effective damage. Swampert also OHKOes Heatran not holding Shuca Berry with Earth Power.

  • Ground-immune Pokemon such as Zapdos, Gyarados, Latios, and Cresselia are obvious partners for Heatran, as they can easily switch into the Ground-type attacks that Heatran hates.

  • Latios is a standout partner for Heatran, as their typings perfectly complement one another defensively. Between the two of them, they resist every type besides Rock. Heatran can easily switch in on Dragon- and Ice-type moves such as Choice Scarf Salamence's Draco Meteor and Abomasnow's Blizzard, which threaten Latios. Additionally, Latios can freely use Life Orb Draco Meteor once Heatran removes opposing Steel-types.

  • Zapdos hits Water-type Pokemon for super effective damage with Thunderbolt or Hidden Power Grass.

  • While Cresselia is one of Heatran's primary targets, they are also one of Heatran's greatest allies. Cresselia has access to the common speed control tools Icy Wind, Trick Room, and Thunder Wave, in addition to Skill Swap + Levitate. Cresselia is also a great counter against Fighting-types such as Infernape and Hariyama, which target Heatran. Depending on the team, Cresselia is a great user of Sunny Day, which boosts the damage of Heat Wave and reduces Water-type damage.

Bulky Support.description:
Set Details

  • Cresselia is a versatile support piece that offers various speed control and damage mitigation options. With excellent bulk and a useful immunity to Ground-type attacks, Cresselia can provide support to nearly everything in the format.

  • Cresselia runs Psychic for reliable STAB-boosted damage. For coverage, Cresselia typically opts for either Ice Beam to hit Latios, Salamence, and Zapdos or Hidden Power Fire to target Metagross, Bronzong, Scizor, Abomasnow, and Shedinja. Icy Wind is an alternative speed control move. When using Icy Wind, Cresselia will usually run at least 116 Speed EVs to outspeed Latios, Gengar, and Infernape after using it.

  • Trick Room and Thunder Wave are Cresselia's main speed control moves. Its immense natural bulk ensures it can survive most unboosted hits in the metagame, providing the longevity needed to safely set up Trick Room or consistently spread paralysis across the opposing team. If Cresselia is using Trick Room, it can opt for minimum Speed to act first under Trick Room conditions.

  • Safeguard is a rarer move on Cresselia used to counter status-spreading Pokemon such as Thunder Wave Cresselia and Dark Void Smeargle. This can be paired with Swagger to grant allies an Attack boost without inflicting confusion. Swagger may also be used with Lum Berry partners and can disrupt the enemy team by fishing for confusion self-hits.

  • Rest allows Cresselia to sustain itself and gives you a potential win condition by letting Cresselia stall the opponent until their Pokemon run out of move PP. Reflect and Protect are moves to deter Explosion, which otherwise can be used to remove Cresselia without much fear.

  • Sitrus Berry gives Cresselia some burst healing to recover from high-power moves. Chesto Berry heals the sleep inflicted by threats such as Dark Void Smeargle and Hypnosis Bronzong as well as enabling Cresselia to use Rest without any drawbacks. Leftovers give Cresselia consistent healing and is commonly used with Protect.

  • The defensive investment allows Cresselia to survive a Self-Destruct from Snorlax or two Life Orb-boosted Draco Meteors from Latios. The Special Attack EVs grant Cresselia the ability to OHKO Salamence with Ice Beam most of the time and consistently 4HKO bulky Metagross with Hidden Power Fire.

Teambuilding

  • Cresselia's best partner is Metagross, as its offensive output more than makes up for Cresselia's lacking damage potential, and it defensively complements Cresselia by resisting Explosion. Metagross greatly benefits from Cresselia's speed control, and by holding a Lum Berry, it can receive an Attack boost from Swagger without inflicting confusion.

  • Heatran and Snorlax also stand out as exemplary Cresselia partners. Cresselia can take on the Fighting-types that threaten both Heatran and Snorlax, offer speed control, or, in Heatran's case, use Skill Swap to replace its Ground-type weakness with an immunity.

  • As Icy Wind Cresselia often runs significant Speed investment, it can take a more offensive role and pairs well with Kangaskhan, which can offer Fake Out support and appreciates Cresselia's Icy Wind speed control. Fast Heatran and Suicune can also fit neatly on teams with Icy Wind Cresselia.

  • Steel-types such as Metagross, Heatran, and Scizor threaten significant damage against Cresselia, while it cannot do much in return.

  • Cresselia can be an easy target for Explosion from Metagross, Snorlax, or Bronzong, as it often lacks Protect.

  • Cresselia is vulnerable to Taunt from Gyarados or Gengar, which shuts down its support moves.

Set Details

  • Gyarados acts as one of the premier support Pokemon in VGC 2009, utilizing its unique combination of stats, typing, moveset, and ability. These traits position it well into much of the meta, allowing it to absorb hits through Intimidate and natural bulk, then deny many common strategies by spreading paralysis with Thunder Wave and using Taunt to shut down Trick Room. Its naturally high Attack stat also means that it can consistently deal damage, making it threatening in its own right.

  • Waterfall is used as a consistent STAB move. It also pairs incredibly well with Thunder Wave; Gyarados can paralyze opposing Pokemon to outspeed them, then use Waterfall's flinch chance to significantly reduce the foe's chance of moving. This combination can allow Gyarados to beat Pokemon that it may otherwise have a poor matchup against.

  • Taunt gives Gyarados the ability to shut down numerous strategies in 2009. Notably, Gyarados is naturally faster than Smeargle, allowing it to Taunt Smeargle before it has the opportunity to use Dark Void. It also prevents dominant strategies like Trick Room and paralysis spam, making Gyarados a consistent answer on any team for a wide variety of matchups.

  • Stone Edge is useful as a coverage move for Gyarados, allowing it to deal significant damage to many of the Pokemon that threaten or resist it normally. This includes Zapdos and Latios, which can hit it with Thunderbolt, as well as opposing Gyarados that can normally take Waterfall hits safely.

  • Wacan Berry helps to alleviate Gyarados's significant weakness to Electric-type moves, and its EVs can be adjusted to let it survive all but the absolute strongest Electric-type moves in the format with it. Sitrus Berry simply adds extra longevity, which can be extremely useful for a Pokemon that often loves to switch in and take hits.

  • This EV spread has a heavy focus on Speed, running Jolly to outspeed maximum Speed Smeargle and Heatran as well as Adamant Kangaskhan. It also survives Modest Zapdos's Thunderbolt with Wacan Berry and always survives a Life Orb Latios Draco Meteor. The rest of the EVs are dumped into Attack.

Teambuilding

  • Gyarados's typing makes it match up well against most of the Steel-types in the format, since it resists both the STAB and common coverage moves of Pokemon like Heatran and Metagross. Its supportive moves in Thunder Wave and Taunt can dominate the speed control of a battle. Thunder Wave enables slower threats like Snorlax and Metagross to attack safely and can help reduce the fear of Speed ties for Pokemon like Latios. Taunt can safely deny setup strategies and Trick Room, allowing the rest of your team to enforce their own strategy.

  • The double weakness to Electric is the central issue of Gyarados, making Zapdos or Latios that run Thunderbolt a significant threat. These threats are what often force Gyarados to run Wacan Berry. Gyarados can also struggle offensively against any Pokemon that resists Water, since it will commonly run Waterfall as its only damaging move. However, Gyarados can sometimes run other coverage moves over one of Taunt or Thunder Wave to mitigate this.

  • The defensive and offensive issues mentioned above are often remedied by the same type of partner: a Dragon-type. Latios and Garchomp both provide safe pivots into Electric-type moves, as well as being able to hit opposing Dragon-types that would resist Gyarados's Water-type attacks. Gyarados also works well with Steel-types on its own team, as it matches up well against the Fire- and Ground-types that threaten them.

DD Sweeper.description:
Set Details

  • Dragon Dance transforms Gyarados from a supportive pivot into an offensive sweeper. Its natural bulk gives it the chance to set up in the face of opposing threats, then outspeed all but the fastest Choice Scarf Pokemon. This allows Gyarados to become an immense issue, as it can often remove the few checks a team has with support and then beat the rest of the team by itself. It can function as either the main win condition of a team or a simple offensive threat that can also punish passive turns from an opponent.

  • Gyarados is forced to choose between one of two coverage moves: Stone Edge and Ice Fang. While boosted, each allows it to beat one of the Pokemon that would normally check it. Stone Edge can OHKO Zapdos, the main Electric-type in the tier, while Ice Fang lets it defeat Dragon-types like Latios and Garchomp consistently. Both can also be useful as an option to hit opposing Gyarados, which otherwise may attempt to stop a sweep with Thunder Wave or Taunt.

  • The item choices are similar to the supportive set, with Sitrus Berry often being swapped for Lum Berry. The goal of these items is to provide Gyarados with a single turn to gain an advantage. Wacan Berry allows it to survive a hit and boost to outspeed Zapdos and Latios, flipping the matchup entirely. Lum Berry gives it a different form of safety, allowing it to safely Dragon Dance without fearing burn or paralysis shutting it down.

  • The EV spread here is focused on outspeeding some of the fastest Pokemon in the tier and knocking out common checks. At +1 Speed, Gyarados outspeeds Raikou, Azelf, and Adamant Choice Scarf Metagross. At +1 Attack, it OHKOs bulky Zapdos with Stone Edge and KOs a standard Latios after Life Orb chip damage with Ice Fang. The defensive EVs allow it to take a Thunderbolt from Jolly Zapdos or Life Orb Latios with Wacan Berry. Note that Gyarados can still run the defensive EV spread while running Dragon Dance if the player is willing to sacrifice OHKOs for the ability to survive Modest Zapdos's Thunderbolt.

Teambuilding

  • Due to being a setup threat, Dragon Dance Gyarados appreciates support from Pokemon with Fake Out or Follow Me. This includes Infernape, Smeargle, and Togekiss. These can make Gyarados extremely hard to stop, as it can target down specific threats to clear the way for a full sweep while opposing Pokemon are forced to focus on the support. Gyarados also still matches up well against the Steel-types common in the meta, as they allow for easy opportunities to set up.

  • The pairing of Zapdos and Garchomp can do significant damage before it Gyarados gets set up through the use of spread moves and force it to choose who to attack. Similarly, other strategies that utilize strong spread moves, like Trick Room or Explosion spam, can be difficult for Gyarados and its supports to deal with.

  • As mentioned above, supports that either give it Gyarados a turn to set up or redirect damage are extremely useful for executing the main strategy of the set. Other Pokemon like Garchomp, Abomasnow, and Rhyperior that resist Electric or Rock can be utilized with it to mitigate the damage from Zapdos and Garchomp and improve its matchup against Trick Room. Rhyperior can also run Lightning Rod to redirect attacks away from Gyarados. These partners either allow Gyarados to safely pivot out and set up at a later time or combine with it to beat threats before they can become issues.

Bulky Attacker.description:
Set Details

  • Snorlax is a slow, bulky bruiser with access to one of the most powerful spread moves in the metagame: STAB-boosted Self-Destruct. Between its massive bulk and single weakness to Fighting, very few Pokemon can beat Snorlax one on one. However, Snorlax is held back by its low Speed, which makes it reliant on Trick Room or Thunder Wave support, and it is susceptible to status moves, Intimidate, and well-timed Protect usage.
  • Frustration is Snorlax's main attacking move to make slow, steady progress, and it's Snorlax's preferred single-target STAB move to thwart the rare Transform Smeargle.
  • Self-Destruct provides enormous spread damage, and Earthquake rounds out Snorlax's coverage, letting it hit Rock- and Steel-types that resist its Normal-type STAB moves. Fire Punch is an alternative coverage move that hits threats such as Metagross, Bronzong, Shedinja, and Scizor. Protect is mandatory to let Snorlax avoid double targeting or allow its ally to set Trick Room.
  • Sitrus Berry is Snorlax's preferred item, giving it burst healing and notably preventing Life Orb Latios from achieving a 2HKO on it with Draco Meteor. Custap Berry is an alternative, letting Snorlax catch foes off-guard with an unexpected priority Self-Destruct.
  • The defensive investment allows Snorlax to survive Close Combat from Infernape, two Meteor Mashs from Adamant Metagross, two Earthquakes from Adamant Life Orb Rhyperior, and three Thunderbolts from Modest Zapdos. The Attack investment grants Snorlax the ability to 3HKO most Cresselia and 2HKO bulky Zapdos with Frustration. The Speed investment lets Snorlax outspeed paralyzed Choice Scarf Smeargle.

Teambuilding

  • Snorlax works well on both bulky goodstuff and hard Trick Room teams. As such, Cresselia is one of its best partners. Cresselia provides Snorlax Trick Room or Thunder Wave support and can beat Fighting-types, while Snorlax can patch up its limited damage output and threaten Steel-types.
  • Snorlax appreciates Ground-immune allies so that it can freely use Earthquake. Zapdos, Bronzong, Latios, and Salamence all fit the bill, with each providing unique upsides. While Zapdos and Bronzong provide speed control in Thunder Wave and Trick Room, respectively, Latios and Salamence handily beat Infernape. Salamence has the added benefit of Intimidate support to patch up Snorlax's low Defense.
  • Snorlax struggles against a handful of common threats. It often can't hit Gengar due to Gengar's Normal and Ground immunities. It also trades damage poorly against Metagross and Rhyperior.
  • Aside from direct damage output, Snorlax is susceptible to status moves such as Cresselia's Thunder Wave, Gengar's Will-O-Wisp, and Smeargle's Dark Void. Repeated triggers of Intimidate from Gyarados and Salamence greatly hamper Snorlax's damage output as well. Finally, Snorlax runs the risk of using Self-Destruct against a foe's Protect, which wastes the move and knocks itself out.

Trick Room Support.description:
Set Details

  • Level 1 Smeargle aims to allow an ally like Bronzong to easily set Trick Room through disruptive means while being as slow as possible under Trick Room, thanks to it being level 1. Smeargle is so frail that it typically never avoids 2HKOs when holding the Focus Sash, so not much is left behind when opting for level 1 aside from Speed. Smeargle often is more than happy to sacrifice itself to allow Trick Room to be set up, allowing a Trick Room sweeper to take its place and maximize the amount of turns it can spend within Trick Room.

  • Since Smeargle's HP at level 1 is so low, Endeavor can be used to bring foes down to extremely low health, allowing allies like Bronzong to easily clean up. Dark Void puts both foes to sleep and is extremely strong in Trick Room, as Smeargle will always be moving first. Detect is an alternative option to Dark Void that may allow Smeargle to survive the first turn as its ally sets up Trick Room and then threaten Endeavor under Trick Room.

  • Follow Me and Fake Out are Smeargle's tools to allow Trick Room to be set up easily. Fake Out can be used into a Pokemon that would normally deny Trick Room, namely Taunt users like Gyarados. Follow Me is able to redirect any attacks that threaten Trick Room setters while also redirecting opposing Fake Out, ensuring that Trick Room goes up no matter what.

  • Focus Sash is absolutely necessary due to level 1 Smeargle being OHKOed by any move that it is not immune to.

  • Smeargle's HP and Speed are as low as possible to ensure that not only is it as slow as possible, but so that Endeavor brings foes to as low of HP as possible.

Teambuilding

  • Level 1 Smeargle belongs specifically on teams that have their main focus on Trick Room; otherwise, level 50 Smeargle would be better thanks to its far higher Speed. Trick Room setters like Cresselia and Bronzong heavily appreciate its ability to use Fake Out and Follow Me to allow them to easily set up Trick Room, while setup sweepers in Trick Room like Snorlax appreciate its ability to redirect attacks or put foes to sleep, making setting up far safer and easier.

  • While Smeargle is able to circumvent many of the ways to deny Trick Room—Taunt, Fake Out, raw power, and some more—it cannot stop Imprison from the likes of Mismagius and Dusknoir, both of which can run both Imprison and Trick Room to ensure that Trick Room never gets set up.

  • Priority attackers such as Scizor, Metagross, and even just Fake Out Pokemon like Infernape are troublesome for Smeargle, as they can move before Smeargle even in Trick Room, easily 2HKOing it thanks to Focus Sash. Spread attackers like Rhyperior are also troublesome, ignoring Follow Me entirely. Ghost-types like Gengar are able to ignore Endeavor, making Smeargle relatively easy to ignore. Gengar also carries Taunt and is immune to Fake Out, making it very easy for Gengar to thwart Smeargle's plans.

  • Trick Room setters like Bronzong are absolutely necessary partners for Smeargle, as it only works alongside those. Bronzong in particular is able to sit in front of the Bullet Punch duo of Scizor and Metagross, as it resists most of their attacks and can put them to sleep with Hypnosis. Additionally, when inside Trick Room, it can threaten Mismagius and Gengar with powerful Gyro Ball. Cresselia is a less common option but fares well into Gengar and Infernape, hitting them with Psychic.

  • Allies like Rhyperior can take advantage of Trick Room while dealing good damage to Bullet Punch Scizor and Metagross, as well as Fake Out users like Infernape. Snorlax is able to use Belly Drum and sweep, taking great advantage of Smeargle's tools in that regard. Metagross is a versatile Pokemon that can reduce its Speed with Hammer Arm to operate in Trick Room, while the likes of Machamp and Spiritomb are more niche options that can threaten the Ghost-types like Gengar, Mismagius, and Dusknoir that annoy Smeargle with Payback and Sucker Punch, respectively. Spiritomb in particular can also set up Trick Room itself.

Bulky Offensive.description:
Set Details

  • Metagross is a strong attacker with a wide movepool, excellent stats, and a beneficial ability in Clear Body, which prevents its stats from being lowered by common effects like Intimidate and Icy Wind. With its Steel / Psychic typing, Metagross has valuable resistances to popular attacks such as Explosion, Draco Meteor, Blizzard, and Rock Slide. Metagross has a Speed stat that allows it to benefit from all forms of speed control, but most notably, it appreciates Thunder Wave and Trick Room support from its teammates. These traits make Metagross a centralizing force in the VGC 2009 metagame that is easy to fit onto many different styles of teams.

  • Meteor Mash is Metagross's strongest damage option, Bullet Punch offers priority to finish off weakened foes, and Earthquake is the best way to trade damage against opposing Metagross while additionally giving coverage for Heatran.

  • Explosion allows Metagross to readily trade itself for an opposing Pokemon and position a more advantageous Pokemon onto the field.

  • Lum Berry is the most popular item for lead Metagross; curing the sleep from Smeargle's Dark Void makes Metagross one of the most consistent answers to Smeargle, as it can easily bypass Smeargle's Focus Sash with Bullet Punch.

  • Shuca Berry allows Metagross to always survive Latios's Life Orb-boosted Hidden Power Ground as well as Earthquake from Trick Room sweepers such as opposing Metagross and Rhyperior. Occa Berry lets Metagross trade favorably against lead Infernape, taking it down to its Focus Sash with Earthquake then following up with a priority Bullet Punch. It also lets Metagross use Earthquake more freely against lead Heatran and deny it a potential Substitute.

  • The bulk EV investment gives Metagross the ability to almost always survive two unboosted Earthquakes from opposing Adamant Metagross and maximizes the chance of surviving two Thunderbolts from Modest Zapdos, while the Attack investment lets it KO Latios with Meteor Mash followed by Bullet Punch most of the time.

Teambuilding

  • Bulky Metagross appreciates speed control from partners such as Cresselia, Bronzong, and Gengar, as without it, Metagross will rarely move first due to middling Speed.

  • Ground-immune partners such as Latios, Cresselia, and Gyarados help to dissuade foes from targeting Metagross with Earthquake and enable Metagross itself to freely use Earthquake without damaging its partner.

  • Snorlax has strong synergy with Metagross, as it's a slow Fire-resistant Pokemon capable of taking on Water-types as well as checking Trick Room strategies.

  • Water-types such as Gyarados, Suicune, Swampert, Kingdra, and Ludicolo resist Metagross's Meteor Mash and threaten Metagross in return with powerful attacks. Zapdos similarly fears little from bulky offensive Metagross and can threaten significant damage.

  • Bronzong can freely set up Trick Room against Metagross, so reliable Trick Room counterplay is required if you're leading with it.

  • Heatran and Infernape threaten to OHKO Metagross if it is not holding the Occa Berry.

Choice Scarf.description:
Set Details

  • With a Choice Scarf, Metagross outspeeds most of the top threats in the VGC 2009 metagame; most notably, it is faster than Latios, Infernape, and Zapdos. Choice Scarf Metagross excels at disrupting the opposing team and dealing crucial chip damage to enable other strong and fast attackers.

  • Iron Head is usually the best choice for Choice Scarf Metagross due to its frequent flinch rate and reliability. Meteor Mash can be used as an alternative to deal more damage.

  • Rock Slide lets Metagross deal strong spread damage, disrupt the opposing team with flinches, and target usual Metagross checks such as Zapdos and Gyarados.

  • With Choice Scarf, Explosion can be used before the opposing Pokemon acts to quickly eliminate them.

  • Trick gives Choice Scarf Metagross the option to cripple an opposing support such as Cresselia or Bronzong, which it would otherwise be powerless against. Alternatively, Earthquake lets Metagross damage opposing Steel-types.

  • With 252 Speed EVs and an Adamant nature, Metagross outspeeds Latios and anything slower while Speed tying with Azelf and Raikou. An Adamant nature is typically preferred over Jolly to maximize damage, but a Jolly nature can be used to consistently outspeed Azelf and Raikou, in addition to Adamant Dragon Dance-boosted Gyarados, Modest Choice Scarf Togekiss, Weavile, Crobat, and Aerodactyl.

Teambuilding

  • Focus Sash Infernape has strong synergy with Choice Scarf Metagross. It threatens opposing Metagross as well as other bulky threats such as Snorlax, and the pairing enables a popular strategy: using Feint with Infernape and Explosion with Metagross on the first turn to immediately clear the opposing Pokemon while also activating Infernape's Blaze.

  • Strong Psychic-types like Latios and Azelf pair nicely with Metagross. Latios is a very effective partner for Choice Scarf Metagross, as it can exploit the chip damage inflicted onto the opposing team by Metagross and KO threats to Metagross that may otherwise be invested to survive Latios's attacks such as Snorlax, Suicune, or Swampert. Azelf forms a potent Explosion spam core with Metagross that can overwhelm unprepared teams. Metagross appreciates that both Pokemon beat Infernape and have Ground immunities with Levitate.

  • Gengar appreciates the disruption of Choice Scarf Metagross, which can enable it to set up Rain Dance, harass opposing support Pokemon with Taunt, or just attack with Shadow Ball. Gengar's Ghost typing makes Explosion a constant threat that the opponent has to play around very carefully.

  • Opposing Metagross threatens significant damage with Earthquake and fears little from Choice Scarf Metagross. Even if Earthquake is used by Choice Scarf Metagross, the damage trade will typically favor a bulky Metagross.

  • Even after using Trick, Choice Scarf Metagross can be vulnerable to Trick Room cores such as Bronzong and Rhyperior. When building with Choice Scarf Metagross, it is essential to ensure the team has reliable Trick Room counterplay.

  • Certain Water-types such as Suicune, Swampert, Empoleon, Ludicolo, and Kingdra are only threatened by Metagross if it uses Explosion, and due to the Choice Scarf, that can be scouted freely on the first turn.

Glass Cannon.description:
Set Details

  • Latios uses its excellent Special Attack and Speed to threaten tremendous damage with Draco Meteor and its coverage moves. Usually kept in the back of the team to clean up weakened foes, Latios demands significant respect in the teambuilder, and as such, Latios's Draco Meteor is often used as the damage benchmark for defensive investment. Latios is one of the most dominant offensive threats in the metagame, and every team needs to be prepared for it.

  • Draco Meteor is by far Latios’s strongest move. It is only resisted by Steel-types, and even a -2 Special Attack Draco Meteor is comparable in power to a neutral Dragon Pulse.

  • Psychic is a consistent STAB move with no drawback or accuracy issues. Thunderbolt provides Latios with a move to hit Metagross, Heatran, Bronzong, and Scizor for neutral damage while also KOing Gyarados if it does not hold a Wacan Berry. Hidden Power Ground offers more damage against Metagross and Heatran; however, it does not hit Bronzong or Scizor.

  • Substitute is a niche option on Life Orb Latios to capitalize on the defensive plays induced by the threat of Draco Meteor. Grass Knot can be used as a coverage move to hit certain threats such as Rhyperior, Swampert, and Suicune for significant damage.

  • Focus Sash gives Latios an advantage in the mirror matchup and the ability to survive many common attacks that would otherwise OHKO it. Holding a Life Orb, Latios receives a crucial damage boost that grants it the ability to achieve important KOs such as Heatran not holding a Shuca Berry with Hidden Power Ground, Smeargle with Psychic, and Rhyperior with Draco Meteor.

Teambuilding

  • Steel-types such as Metagross, Heatran, and Bronzong have excellent type synergy with Latios and can combat common Latios checks such as Cresselia, Metagross, Snorlax, and Weavile.

  • Snorlax is a notable partner for Latios with good type synergy, low Speed to operate in Trick Room conditions, and the ability to break through Latios checks with Self-Destruct. In turn, Latios takes on the Fighting-types that threaten Snorlax.

  • Latios is vulnerable if it fails to KO the foe before it moves, so especially bulky Pokemon such as Metagross, Heatran, Bronzong, Snorlax, Cresselia, and Togekiss are very effective checks to Latios. Equally, exceptionally fast Pokemon like Choice Scarf Metagross, Weavile, and Yanmega can act first to shut Latios down.

  • Enemy speed control is particularly impactful to Latios, as it is unable to weather attacks under Trick Room conditions or if it is paralyzed by Thunder Wave.

  • Latios is almost always walled by Shedinja; teams with Latios must respect Shedinja or face losing in the teambuilder.

Choice Item.description:
Set Details

  • With a Choice item, Latios can overwhelm its usual checks either by acting first due to Choice Scarf or with the power boost from Choice Specs. Using Trick can cripple an enemy support Pokemon such as Bronzong or Cresselia and, in some situations, shut down dangerous setup threats such as Calm Mind Suicune and Curse Snorlax.

  • Dragon Pulse is preferred over Psychic to target common threats such as Latios, Cresselia, and Garchomp without locking into Draco Meteor. Protect is an alternative move to be used after using Trick to give away its Choice item; it is more popular when Latios holds Choice Scarf and particularly when playing with item swaps between games. Protect may be used to stall the turns of Trick Room after giving away the Choice Scarf to a Trick Room setter.

  • Choice Scarf Latios outspeeds key threats including Azelf, Raikou, and Weavile in addition to Choice Scarf Metagross, Smeargle, Togekiss, Zapdos, and Garchomp. Choice Specs Latios can OHKO Snorlax with Helping Hand-boosted Draco Meteor, 2HKO Metagross with Thunderbolt, and OHKO Zapdos with Draco Meteor.

  • Choice Scarf Latios typically prefers a Modest nature to maximize damage. Choice Specs Latios runs Timid to outspeed Garchomp and Infernape and to Speed tie with Gengar and Latios.

Teambuilding

  • Metagross stands out as the best partner for both Choice Scarf and Choice Specs Latios, as it can assist with breaking through threats such as Cresselia and Snorlax and deal crucial chip damage with Bullet Punch to finish off weakened foes like Infernape, Smeargle, Latios, and Weavile holding a Focus Sash.

  • Choice Specs Latios is very often paired with a Fake Out lead such as Infernape, Kangaskhan, Smeargle, or Weavile to capitalize on its immediate power by shutting down the enemy for one turn. Infernape in particular excels at eliminating the Steel-types like Metagross, Bronzong, and Heatran that naturally resist the Dragon-type moves that Choice Specs Latios relies on.

  • When holding a Choice item, Latios is less flexible and so can be taken advantage of by enemy threats that can survive a Draco Meteor such as Metagross, Cresselia, Snorlax, Heatran, and Bronzong. When using a more offensive team, it is important to be cautious when using Trick on Cresselia, as it can be an immense threat with a Choice Scarf if your team lacks reliable Cresselia checks such as Metagross and Snorlax.

Trick Room Support.description:
Set Details

  • Bronzong enables bulky Trick Room sweepers by consistently setting Trick Room and supporting them with boosts and disruption. Its excellent typing and ability prevent it from being KOed by common high-power moves such as Metagross's Meteor Mash, Latios's Draco Meteor, Earthquake, Explosion, and Self-Destruct.

  • This support-oriented Bronzong often uses Iron Head as its only attack due to a high flinch rate and the STAB boost. Psychic is an alternative attack that is not damped by Intimidate's Attack drops and deals with common Iron Head-resistant foes such as Zapdos, Gyarados, and Infernape.

  • Swagger acts as both a boosting and disruption tool. It boosts the Attack of teammates carrying Lum Berry or Persim Berry so that they can achieve OHKOs. It disrupts opposing special attackers by confusing them in exchange for a useless Attack boost, and it can be used with risk against physical attackers.

  • The final support move depends on the held item. Substitute with Leftovers ensures Bronzong can take at least one hit after Trick Room expires so that it can set Trick Room again. Rest and Chesto Berry prolong Bronzong's presence, so it can continue supporting its team and can win favorable late-game scenarios solo. Light Screen reduces damage from many special attacks such as Thunderbolt that neutrally hit Bronzong and its common partners.

  • Alternative moves include Reflect to bolster the team's physical bulk, Safeguard to prevent status effects, Rock Slide for its flinch chance against both foes, and Skill Swap to give Levitate to teammates that are weak to Ground.

  • Leftovers passively prolongs Bronzong's board presence and has excellent synergy with Substitute and damage-free turns caused by Iron Head's flinches and confusion self-hits. Chesto Berry allows instant full-HP healing from Rest, frees the valuable Lum Berry for teammates, and stops Dark Void and Hypnosis from preventing Trick Room setup. Occa Berry allows Bronzong to survive Typhlosion's Eruption and Sun-boosted Fire-type Heat Wave.

  • The given EVs ensure Bronzong survives Adamant Choice Band Metagross's Explosion and Infernape's Overheat. A minimum Speed IV and a negative-Speed nature are used so that Bronzong almost always moves first when Trick Room is set up.

Teambuilding

  • Bronzong has positive or neutral matchups against the three top-tier Pokemon in the format: Latios, Cresselia, and Metagross. It appreciates teammates that resist Fire, Electric, and Water and deal meaningful damage. Most slow, bulky Pokemon benefit from Bronzong setting Trick Room for them.

  • Rhyperior is Bronzong's strongest partner. It moves before most Pokemon when Trick Room is active, moves after Bronzong under Trick Room so that it can receive Swagger's Attack boost right before it attacks, does not hit Bronzong with Earthquake due to Levitate, resists Fire, and OHKOes common Bronzong checks such as Heatran, Zapdos, and Gyarados. Snorlax shares similar traits to Rhyperior, and while it doesn't has as much synergy with Bronzong as Rhyperior does, it has fewer polarizing matchups.

*A level 1 Smeargle both enables Bronzong to set Trick Room safely and becomes a massive threat once Trick Room is set. While Latios does not work well under Trick Room, it has excellent type synergy with Bronzong. Togekiss and Hariyama both apply board pressure with Air Slash flinches and Guts-boosted attacks respectively, and enable Trick Room setting using Follow Me or Fake Out, respectively.

  • Taunt makes Bronzong almost useless by shutting down its incredible support moves. Trick + Choice Scarf disables Bronzong by locking Bronzong into Trick Room the turn it sets the field condition up and preventing move swapping in later parts of the battle.

  • Fire-types both resist Iron Head and 2HKO Bronzong with STAB Fire-type moves. The most common, Heatran, can block Swagger with Substitute. Electric- and Water-types such as Zapdos, Gyarados, and Suicune resist Iron Head and can wear down Bronzong with strong STAB attacks.

Offensive Support.description:
Set Details

  • Toxicroak is an offensive and supportive Pokemon that threatens solid damage while providing Fake Out and disruption support with Taunt, making it excel as a lead. It is a solid Fighting-type sweeper that pairs exceptionally well with Pokemon that use Surf due to a lack of accurate Water-type attacks, like Ludicolo and Empoleon, thanks to Dry Skin.

  • Cross Chop is Toxicroak's most powerful Fighting-type STAB attack and has the fewest drawbacks. Cross Chop even allows Toxicroak to OHKO Snorlax with a critical hit.

  • Sucker Punch is a reliable priority move that lets Toxicroak threaten to 2HKO Latios and Focus Sash Gengar. Sucker Punch is also a great coverage move and synergizes well with Taunt.

  • Taunt is used to disrupt the likes of Cresselia, Smeargle, and Bronzong, preventing them from causing mayhem with moves such as Thunder Wave, Swagger, Dark Void, and Trick Room.

  • Toxicroak uses Focus Sash to increase its longevity, helping it pick up KOs or support its partners for longer.

Teambuilding

  • Toxicroak is particularly good at threatening Snorlax with Cross Chop and winning against Latios and Gengar one-on-one with Sucker Punch. It also excels at disrupting Bronzong and Smeargle with Taunt.

  • Toxicroak struggles severely against Metagross, as it cannot deal meaningful damage while Metagross can easily 2HKO it with Meteor Mash or Earthquake and bypass its Focus Sash with Bullet Punch. Cresselia is also very difficult for Toxicroak to damage, since it can threaten a KO with Psychic despite Toxicroak disrupting its support capabilities.

  • Toxicroak is mostly paired with Water-type sweepers that run Surf, like Ludicolo and Empoleon, on rain teams. Dry Skin provides both an immunity to Surf and healing. These Water-types generally deal solid damage, but in particular, they easily threaten Rhyperior. Toxicroak also excels as a counter to opposing rain strategies and Water-types.

  • Outside of the Water-types, Metagross is a great partner capable of wearing down Cresselia with Meteor Mash, and it has an even matchup against opposing Metagross.
